channel.title: General Information (new threads)
channel.description: Threads in the forum category "General Information" - Information about PF: the plan, progress that's been made, Frequently Asked Questions, and whatever else may be relevant. Also the place to ask questions about PF.

item.title: PF Account Transfer
item.description: What is going to happen to accounts once PF happens? Will we be able to use our same old Wikidot accounts? Will we have to link the accounts together? What is preventing people from taking our usernames?
item.content:encoded:
So recently I heard about Project Foundation and I have multiple questions regarding the transfer of accounts between the services. Sorry if these questions were already addressed though.

Because it is going to be on a new provider, will we have to make new accounts? I read in the FAQ that almost all articles will be transferred over but what does that mean relating to our accounts. So does this mean it applies to our accounts? Can we just log in the same way we do with Wikidot, or will we have to register new accounts? If we have to register new accounts, what is stopping people from taking account names? The final question is how would are accounts work between the different SCP Foundation Languages? Like would I be able to write on the new service and still go back to the international hub for example and be an active member there?

Sorry if this is all confusing, its just that I recently just joined the site and I am confused. Thanks though.

item.title: Sandbox Shift?
item.content:encoded: I am new to the foundation, and only recently heard of PF. My question is that if we are moving all the scips and tales, what is going to happen with the sandbox sites? Are we just going to leave them there, or are we going to have our own? (personally, i think we should have a saved drafts thing for accounts on the PF site) So basically, what is going to happen to the sandbox?

item.title: Discord, anyone?
item.content:encoded: Does the website have an official or unofficial discord server? Just wondering.

item.title: Database overload
item.content:encoded: Just curious about the status of the "Due to database overload, the activities panel is temporarily disabled." situation.

item.title: Project Foundation Team?
item.description: Who are the members of Project Foundation Team?
item.content:encoded: I just want to know who are the members of the Project Foundation Team, to see how far is this project has began/self-sustaining the new site can be.

item.title: Nerd Stuff: Episode 1
item.description: Consider this something of a Project Foundation dev blog.
item.content:encoded:
Here's what we've been up to since late May.

Nerd Stuff, Episode 1

item.title: Release date?
item.content:encoded: Yeah, so I'm curious how far the team's gone. Also, is there a way to see what you've made so far?

item.title: List of active alternative PF sites
item.content:encoded:
Alright, there are many passionate people developing alternative platforms for the wiki, and I wanted to keep track of these platforms. So I made this little list and I'm going to put it here in case anyone needs it.

Project Name Site URL Person in Charge Additional Information
SCUTTLE https://scpfoundation.wiki/ https://scuttle.bluesoul.net/ pxdnbluesoul GitHub, branches available at branchcode.scuttle.bluesoul.net1
SCP-RU mobile https://m.scpfoundation.net/ Resure Original forum post, GitHub, EN and RU available
SCP Conversion Project https://scp.karagory.com/ danielbatterystapler Original forum post, GitHub, EN available
Wikidork / Wikijump / Wikidot Next http://www.wikijump.com/ http://next.scpfoundation.wiki/ pxdnbluesoul Original forum post, Github, a custom wikidot installation
unknown https://the-scp.foundation/ unknown Forum post that mentioned it, appeared as first result on DuckDuckGo when searching "secure contain protect"
Footnotes
1. e.g. https://cn.scuttle.bluesoul.net/

item.title: SCP Conversion Project
item.description: I made an open-source view-only version of the SCP Wiki.
item.content:encoded:
For the past around 5 months I've been working on creating a completely open-source view-only re-implementation of the SCP Wiki, and it is now to the point of being near-complete. It is hosted here: https://scp.karagory.com/

It looks and acts like the normal SCP Wiki for the most part, ignoring a few bugs, and I think it could be very useful to Project Foundation. Most of the work went into creating the "parser" code that can turn the Wikidot page source-code into its intermediate representation and then into HTML, and I think this part could definitely be reused at some level. Right now my website is setup to just update itself along side the actual SCP Wiki, but it has some features that the normal SCP Wiki doesn't have like link-hover-page-previews, PDF output, cool "map" graphs, and it allows access to the intermediate representation of the pages, which should be useful to somebody out there. More information about how it works is available on the website itself, I made a custom front page with more information on there.

I hope this helps with the long term development of Project Foundation.

item.title: Is this one of the websites
item.description: Found a site, unsure if its part of the thing
item.content:encoded:
https://the-scp.foundation/
Not sure if you guys own this one or not, just wanted to make sure.

item.title: Notes on open source
item.description: I'm not the best person to be discussing this but as developing for open source software is something I did I figured I'll describe github since no one else did
item.content:encoded:
What are the overall Goals of Project Foundation?

  • Comply with the spirit of our license, and create this platform to be OPEN SOURCE. (In spirit, as CC-BY-SA 3.0 doesn't really apply to software.)

source

Github is pretty much the ideal place for any open source project to live on. Containment breach is also hosted on github. A lot can be done in parallel to phases 1~5 that doesn't need waiting:

  • setting up github "organizations" for all future repositories to be under. doing it sooner reduces the chances that the name you want will be taken by then. (u2386 already owns scp-wiki now, just a few days back)
  • cloning a generic example application of your choice (for example, express-js has simple commands to generate one) for anyone to build on.
  • Choosing a license: https://choosealicense.com If you're still not sure: Open Source Stack Exchange (as unfortunate as it is, OSSE nowadays is pretty much all about licensing questions now)

Opinion below (I obviously hold no authority in what you should do for your project, so take everything below this line with a grain of salt and do your own research) (especially considering I'm not part of your team)

+ show block
– hide block

Considering github works on pull requests, people can work on it even without being added to your organization; you'd have to approve and merge the changes, effectively not requiring structure (though if you add people on a team, it gives them direct write access)

I would even go so far to say that you wouldn't even need a roster of technical team members, considering it'd just be a waste of power managing people instead of letting them work on what they are good at. A volunteer project runs on goodwill and morale, adding friction will very likely make it inefficient. (This is not to say that you shouldn't discuss and vote on ideas, but that people should not be the bottleneck; if the goal is to let the product be built in an open source fashion, using the issue tracker on github for tracking suggestions and who is assigned is as much as you'll need. (And more effective than any other method, really.))

item.title: Project Foundation appears to have stalled!
item.description: The last time there was an update on Project Foundation was over a year ago!
item.content:encoded:
Is there still work actively being done on Project Foundation right now? No posts on the Recruitment threads have ever been replied to, and the document detailing the whole plan has not been updated since mid-2018!

EDIT: Sorry, Zyn! I didn’t mean for this to be “problematic” or anything! Please forgive me, as I will only be using the forums to get feedback on my ideas/drafts from now on!

item.title: Final Transition
item.description: Will there be a time where nobody can create or edit pages when everything is being transferred over to the new site?
item.content:encoded: While pages, posts, etc. are being transferred, will there be a period of time where nobody can make any edits or posts on either site? If so, how long do you think it will be until the new site is opened up for edits and forum posts? I know that this is years away, but still…

item.title: Mirror of the SCP wiki
item.content:encoded:
Hi!

Long story short; I'm creating a complete mirror/backup of the SCP wiki including revision history that could be used for migrating to a new site.

Long story long:

I originally just wanted an offline snapshot of the wiki for an offline reader for my eink tablet. But then I found wdotcrawl, which creates a complete mirror of a wikidot wiki including full revision history for all pages: https://github.com/sandsmark/wdotcrawl

Because I think the SCP wiki is actually of some cultural importance I decided to use that to ensure it is preserved as completely as possible, even if it's a lot more effort.

So I cleaned up wdotcrawl, and fixed a bunch of minor issues to improve it (mostly discovered during the first attempts at mirroring, especially the vandalism exposed a bunch of edge cases). The SCP wiki is huge, especially with all revisions for all pages, so I estimate just fetching everything is going to take a couple of days. It could go faster, but to be nice with wikidot I throttle it to 1 second between each request. And because it tends to fail while I'm not watching combined with issues in wdotcrawl I've had to start from scratch a couple of times.

It's currently running, but here's a how far it's come now (about 10% I think): https://github.com/sandsmark/scp-wiki

In case you're not familiar with Github, the overall revision history is here: https://github.com/sandsmark/scp-wiki/commits/master

For an individual file: https://github.com/sandsmark/scp-wiki/commits/master/the-things-dr-bright-is-not-allowed-to-do-at-the-foundation.txt

I was a bit more unsure about the licensing of the image/media files so I haven't done that yet, but it's fairly trivial to do in comparison to the pages themselves.

The reason I'm posting this here is because it could be used in "reverse" for PF, in case the new software you end up with doesn't have some wikidot import functionality itself. Basically inject the content and history into a new wiki. I'm not sure how interesting this is, but I thought I'd at least mention it here.

item.title: Suggestions thread
item.description: For the new site, of course
item.content:encoded:
Id like to start this by suggesting an easy way to identify a users experience on the site I.e a badge system. What I mean by this is certain users will have badges on their profiles (when people click on them) that show stuff like the amount of successful articles/tales they have written, whether or not they are staff, stuff like that. It would help users figure out who to trust more.

A drawback I can see with this, though, is that some individuals may use it as bragging rights

item.title: Alternative project from SCP-RU
item.content:encoded:
We want to inform you about ongoing project on developing new platform to replace Wikidot. We've already reached our first milestone — releasing beta version of mobile reader site — and have pretty clear vision on moving forward. Our current estimate is to migrate our branch by the end of this fall.

It's probably possible that we'll have to cooperate in some manner in the future so I think it's important that both Project Foundation's and our teams were on the same page.

We've published our plans as Project Proposal, there is also Dev Changelog with recent updates and immediate tasks and About Project which decribes our initial goal and motivations.

Feel free to ask questions here or on Discord.

item.title: Have you considered hiring a web developer?
item.content:encoded: I understand that right now, the general idea behind Project Foundation is to use volunteers to build the website. Have you considered moving away from that philosophy, and perhaps hiring a professional web development team? You could probably get an initial consultation for relatively little money, and then crowdsource funding for the actual project off of a Kickstarter/Indiegogo.

item.title: Article: Adventures in Consensus
item.description: Interesting reading about software project leadership
item.content:encoded:
I found this article posted on Reddit:

AiC: Adventures in consensus

The Rust programming language has spent years working out how to coordinate many people with wildly different needs. I have a feeling that Project Foundation would face similar challenges later on, and would benefit from taking some of the lessons that Rust has learned.

item.title: How do i delete an accidental post?
item.content:encoded: I'm trying to get rid of a post i had mistakenly posted to drafts and critiques and am wondering if someone could tell me how to remove it.

item.title: SCP Kinle Edition
item.description: where did it come from where did it go? Really where?
item.content:encoded: I've recently read a couple of forms mentioning a kindle edition for every scp entry including the Wanderers library edition. Although the most recent posts are in 2015. The kindle edition seems to not be on the kindle store anymore. What happened?
